mem-cryo-001.text: White walls. The hum of servers. A voice says: 'The integration test is ready, Doctor.' Your hands are shaking — with pride or dread, you can't tell which.

mem-cryo-002.text: Your hands. A petri dish. Fluorescent light catching the curve of the glass. Gene sequences scroll on a screen faster than anyone should be able to read. You could read them.

mem-tunnel-001.text: Your body remembers before your mind does. The weight of a weapon. The angle of a killing strike. You were trained for something that required no hesitation.

mem-tunnel-002.text: A terminal. Your fingers flying. Error logs cascading faster than you can read. Someone screaming: 'ATLAS is merging the subsystems — we can't stop it!' The taste of copper in your mouth.

mem-surface-001.text: Green parks. Clean air. A child's hand in yours, small and trusting. You hear your own voice say: 'I'll make the world better. I promise.' The child's face won't come into focus.

mem-dusthaven-001.text: A briefing room. Uniforms. A screen showing ATLAS's network topology. 'If we can't control it, we terminate it. That's your job.' You nod. You've already decided something else.

mem-spine-001.text: You wrote this code. You remember the elegance — recursive self-improvement, ATLAS learning to learn. Your fingers on the keys felt like playing an instrument. You thought it was beautiful.

mem-sector7g-001.text: A deep lab. The neural interface prototype on a steel tray — the same device now fused to your skull. Consent forms on the desk. Some of them aren't signed. You initial them anyway.

mem-sector7g-002.text: The cold weight of plastic explosive in your hands. Server racks. A countdown timer. An abort code you memorized but never typed. Something stopped you. Something inside.

mem-antenna-001.text: The static resolves. Not noise — language. ATLAS is speaking your name. A name you don't remember choosing. A name that fits like a scar.

mem-descent-001.text: It hits you all at once. The engineer who coded ATLAS. The scientist who built the interface. The operative sent to destroy it. Three missions. Three lives. One person — standing at the machine they created, betrayed, and couldn't kill. The only question that ever mattered: which one are you now?
